metadata.title: United States Language Feature Catalog
metadata.note: This catalog defines the language features used by the map. It combines phonetic shifts, phonetic convergence, and lexical diffusion categories. Replace illustrative descriptions/intensities with measured values when empirical data is available.

categories.label: Phonetic shift
categories.description: A long-term regional sound change, usually measured through pronunciation or acoustic data.

categories.label: Phonetic convergence
categories.description: A sound change that reduces older regional contrasts, such as merging previously distinct vowel classes.

categories.label: Lexical diffusion
categories.description: The spread of words, slang, spelling variants, or terminology across social and geographic networks.

categories.label: Dialect region
categories.description: A broad area where multiple linguistic features cluster together. Boundaries are approximate, not hard borders.

features.name: Northern Cities Vowel Shift
features.plain_language_summary: A chain shift associated with many Inland North cities, involving changes to vowels in words such as cat, cot, bet, bit, and bus.
features.map_behavior: Represent as clustered regional points with high intensity in Inland North urban centers. Use gradual timeline changes rather than rapid jumps.

features.name: Southern Vowel Shift
features.plain_language_summary: A major Southern chain shift involving features such as glide weakening in words like my and hide and related front-vowel changes.
features.map_behavior: Represent as a broad Southern region with varying city intensity rather than a single origin point.

features.name: Low-Back Merger / Cot-Caught Merger
features.plain_language_summary: A merger in which words like cot and caught, or don and dawn, are pronounced the same by many speakers.
features.map_behavior: Represent as a broad convergence gradient, with points showing local merger strength.

features.name: Online Lexical Innovation
features.plain_language_summary: The spread of new words or written variants through online and cultural networks, often jumping between highly connected cities instead of moving steadily across space.
features.map_behavior: Represent with origin points, metro-to-metro edges, and fast timeline animation.

features.name: Mid-Atlantic Lexical Innovation Zone
features.plain_language_summary: An independent zone of written or lexical innovation radiating from Mid-Atlantic metro clusters.
features.map_behavior: Represent as hub points linked to nearby urban corridors.
